source.name: SSA Canonical Catalog
notes: BBQ-friendly smoked fish using a dry brine + pellicle for better smoke adhesion.
Designed for salmon, trout, or firm white fish (sablefish/black cod, mackerel, etc.).
Includes both hot-smoke (ready-to-eat) and warm-smoke guidance, plus sauce pairing ideas.

yield.notes: 6 servings (about 4–6 oz cooked fish per person), depending on cut and appetites.

ingredients.label: Fish fillets (salmon, trout, or firm white fish)
ingredients.notes: Skin-on preferred for salmon/trout. For white fish, choose thicker fillets for easier smoking.

ingredients.label: Kosher salt
ingredients.notes: Dry brine. If using fine/table salt, reduce to ~2 tsp.

ingredients.label: Brown sugar
ingredients.notes: Balances salt and helps browning; optional but recommended.

ingredients.label: Cayenne (optional)
ingredients.notes: A pinch for heat; skip for mild.

ingredients.label: Neutral oil (for brushing rack or fish)
Smoking wood (chunks or chips)
ingredients.notes: Alder is classic for salmon; apple/cherry are mild-sweet; pecan is richer. Avoid heavy mesquite for fish unless used lightly.

ingredients.label: BBQ sauce (optional, for glazing)
ingredients.notes: Use a thinner, tangy sauce; optional glaze late in cook.

ingredients.label: Apple cider vinegar (optional spritz)
ingredients.notes: For a light spritz during smoking to keep surface tacky; optional.

steps.title: Prep the fish
steps.body: Pat fish dry with paper towels. Remove pin bones (for salmon/trout) if needed. Keep skin on if present. Place fish on a wire rack set over a baking sheet.

steps.title: Dry brine
steps.body: Mix kosher salt, brown sugar (if using), black pepper, paprika, garlic powder, onion powder, and cayenne (if using). Sprinkle evenly over fish (top and sides). Refrigerate uncovered 45–60 minutes.

steps.title: Rinse lightly and dry (optional but recommended)
steps.body: For a cleaner smoke flavor, quickly rinse the surface of the fish under cool water to remove excess cure, then pat very dry. Return fish to the rack.

steps.title: Form the pellicle (tacky surface for better smoke)
steps.body: Refrigerate the fish uncovered 30–60 minutes until the surface feels slightly tacky. This helps smoke adhere and improves texture.

steps.title: Preheat smoker
steps.body: Preheat smoker to 200–225°F. Oil the grates lightly. Use a mild wood (alder, apple, cherry). Keep smoke thin and steady—fish takes smoke fast.

steps.title: Smoke the fish
steps.body: Place fish on the smoker, skin-side down if applicable. Smoke at 200–225°F until the thickest part reaches an internal temp of 135°F for salmon (juicy) or 145°F for firm white fish / USDA doneness. Expect ~60–120 minutes depending on thickness. Optional: spritz lightly with apple cider vinegar every 30 minutes if the surface looks dry (not mandatory).

steps.title: Optional BBQ glaze (last 10–15 minutes)
steps.body: If glazing, brush a thin layer of BBQ sauce on the fish during the final 10–15 minutes of smoking so it sets without burning. Keep it light—fish can get overpowered.

steps.title: Rest and serve
steps.body: Remove fish and rest 5–10 minutes. Serve with lemon wedges. Great as a BBQ plate protein or flaked into sandwiches, tacos, salads, or rice bowls.

sauces.label: Vinegar Pepper Sauce
sauces.notes: Especially good with smoked white fish.

refrigerate.instructions: Cool completely, then refrigerate in an airtight container.

freeze.instructions: Wrap tightly and freeze up to 2 months. Thaw overnight in fridge for best texture.

storage.notes: Smoked fish is excellent cold the next day. For reheating, warm gently (avoid drying).

serveModes.label: BBQ Plate
serveModes.notes: Serve with slaw + beans or grilled veg.

serveModes.label: Tacos
serveModes.notes: Flake smoked fish into tortillas with slaw + vinegar sauce.

serveModes.label: Sandwich
serveModes.notes: Use a soft bun, add slaw, and a light aioli or tangy sauce.

notes: Salmon/trout: 130–135°F for juicy (carryover can take it slightly higher).
If you prefer firmer, smoke to 145°F for salmon as well.
Best woods for fish: alder (classic), apple, cherry, maple.
Use pecan lightly for richer smoke. Avoid heavy mesquite unless very small amounts.
Carolina-style: skip sweet glaze; finish with vinegar-pepper sauce + lemon.
KC-style: very light glaze late in cook, then serve extra sauce on the side.
Spicy: add cayenne to rub and a dash of hot sauce to serving sauce.
